results.possibleIdentity: Joseph (Joe) Zorskie is an experimental physicist and educator active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He earned his doctorate from the Stevens Institute of Technology, where his thesis focused on plasma physics. He developed a diagnostic device known as a "plasma scope," which used a plastic scintillator and cameras to photograph the density distribution and fine structure of plasma filaments. During the late 1960s, he served as an Assistant Professor of Physics, with affiliations listed at the New York Institute of Technology and the Stevens Institute of Technology. Zorskie was a resident of Weehawken, New Jersey, and was identified in the technical service records of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) as an expert in holograms, optics, and lasers. Beyond his experimental research, Zorskie became a significant figure in the community surrounding the philosopher J. Krishnamurti and the theoretical physicist David Bohm. He served as a physics teacher at the Brockwood Park School in Hampshire, England, and was a close associate of David Bohm, assisting in the organization, transcription, and editing of the Bohm Seminars in Ojai, California. In his later years, he focused on the connections between mind, brain, and consciousness, contributing to projects such as the Museum of Consciousness and writing on the material nature of conscious experience.
